text.title: Structural Antipatterns and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Common structural mistakes LLMs make when generating Go project layouts: pkg/ misuse, cyclic imports, missing worker shutdown.

antipattern.description: Creating a pkg/ directory for internal helpers, business logic, or service code that no external module imports.
antipattern.reason: pkg/ signals public API. Once there, consumers depend on it. Renaming to internal/ is a breaking change. Reject pkg/ without a named external consumer.

antipattern.description: Package A imports B, package B imports A — produces compile error: import cycle not allowed.
antipattern.reason: Cyclic imports reveal a design flaw: shared logic should move to a third package that neither A nor B owns.

example.code: // BAD: handler imports service, service imports handler (for error types) // GOOD: define error types in internal/apperror, import from both

rule.statement: Every long-running goroutine (queue consumer, ticker, watcher) must select on a quit channel or context cancellation. srv.Shutdown alone does not stop background goroutines.
rule.rationale: Agents generate worker loops without exit conditions. Under SIGTERM, srv.Shutdown returns immediately but the goroutine leaks, processing messages against a closed DB pool.

section.title: Dockerfile Safety

rule.statement: Dockerfile must include USER nobody:nobody and use a distroless or minimal base image for the runtime stage. Never run Go binaries as root in production containers.
rule.rationale: Agents emit Dockerfiles without USER directives, leaving the binary running as root — a container escape vulnerability.

section.reference: Multi-stage distroless Dockerfile for Go services
